name: Blood & Guts
creator: Dorian Yates
summary: Dorian Yates' Blood & Guts HIT as filmed at Temple Gym in the 1996 documentary: one progressive warm-up pyramid then a single all-out working set per exercise to absolute failure and beyond with a partner. Body parts once every six days on a four-day training week (two on, one off, two on, rest). Weekly calendar order varies across interviews; this collection follows the Blood & Guts DVD session order and exercise selections.
principles: One maximum-intensity working set per exercise — quality over quantity.
Warm-ups fire up the muscle but never approach failure (typically 50% then 75% of working weight).
Train each body part once every six days; recovery is when growth happens.
Control the negative, drive the positive with precision (not sloppy momentum), full range of motion.
Every rep has three phases — positive, static hold, and negative — and each can be pushed past normal limits.
Beyond failure with a training partner: forced reps, rest-pause, negatives, partials, and iso-holds.
Log every workout; progressive overload is non-negotiable.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 4 training days in a repeating 7-day cycle (2 on, 1 off, 2 on, 1 off)
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once every 6 days (as stated in the Blood & Guts documentary).
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 1-3 progressive warm-up sets on the first exercise (50% then 75%); 1 warm-up on later exercises when the muscle is already warm — never near failure.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Exactly one all-out working set per exercise to muscular failure, then beyond with forced reps, rest-pause, negatives, partials, or iso-holds. Sessions run 45-60 minutes.

sources.title: Blood & Guts — Full Documentary (Temple Gym, Birmingham)
Blood & Guts — Chest & Biceps (Dorian Yates Nutrition)
Blood & Guts — Back & Rear Delts (Dorian Yates Nutrition)
Blood & Guts — Shoulders & Triceps (Dorian Yates Nutrition)
Blood & Guts — Every. Last. Rep. (Dorian Yates Nutrition)
Blood And Guts Program — The Legacy of HIT (DY Nutrition)

name: Heavy Duty
creator: Mike Mentzer
summary: Mike Mentzer's Heavy Duty is the purest expression of High-Intensity Training (HIT): a handful of exercises, exactly one working set taken to absolute momentary muscular failure (often extended with forced/negative reps or a static hold), and long rest periods between infrequent workouts. Mentzer argued that overtraining - not undertraining - is the bodybuilder's biggest mistake.
principles: Intensity is inversely proportional to volume and frequency: the harder you train, the less you can and should do.
One set to true momentary muscular failure per exercise is not just sufficient - it is optimal.
Only the last, impossible rep recruits the highest-threshold fibers that trigger growth.
Recovery is a finite resource; every extra set or session draws from it and delays growth.
Rest at least 4-7 days between sessions, adding more rest days if progress stalls.
Progress by double progression: add reps within the range, then add weight.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: About twice a week (train roughly every 4th day)
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once every 4-7+ days; the full rotation can span two weeks.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 1-3 progressive warm-up sets per exercise, never approaching failure.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Exactly 1 all-out working set to momentary muscular failure, sometimes extended with forced reps, negatives, or a static hold. Pre-exhaust supersets pair an isolation into a compound.

splitOverview.focus: Rest 4-7+ days, then repeat the rotation

sources.title: Heavy Duty: The Ideal Routine - MikeMentzer.org
Mike Mentzer's Ideal Routine: A Heavy Duty Blueprint - MikeMentzer.org
The Consolidation Program — MikeMentzer.org
Heavy Duty College (official YouTube)
Mike Mentzer's Consolidated Routine - Liftosaur

name: High-Volume Powerbuilding
creator: Ronnie Coleman
summary: Ronnie Coleman — 8× Mr. Olympia — built historic mass on heavy compounds plus mountains of accessory volume, typically six days a week with large groups twice. Official RCSS framing: lift as heavy as possible for as many quality sets and reps as possible, with a hardcore Metroflex mindset. Documented splits commonly run back/bis, chest/tris, legs, shoulders/traps on a weekly rotation.
principles: Heavy compound barbell movements (squat, deadlift, bench, row) are the core of every session.
Work in the 8-12 range with the heaviest weight you can control - and 4-8 on the main lifts.
Volume drives growth: 4-6 exercises and 3-5 sets per body part.
High frequency: train each muscle group twice per week over a 6-day rotation.
Progressive overload through load and total volume, not intensity gimmicks or drop sets.
Train through it - Coleman rarely planned deloads; recovery came from food and sleep.

guidelines.warmupProtocol: 2+ progressive warm-up sets on heavy lifts (e.g. 30% then 60% of your working weight).
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3-5 working sets per exercise; 16-25 total working sets per large muscle group per session (Ronnie Coleman Nutrition guide). Warm-ups extra.

sources.title: Ronnie Coleman Workout Guide — Ronnie Coleman Nutrition
Ronnie Coleman Signature Series — How to be Hardcore
Ronnie Coleman Workout Program - Fitness Volt

name: Harder Than Last Time (HTLT)
creator: Greg Doucette
summary: Greg Doucette's 'Harder Than Last Time' system is built on progressive overload, controlled tempo, and long-term sustainability rather than any single magic split. Every muscle is trained at least twice a week, reps are done slowly for maximum time under tension, and you target rep ranges (not fixed numbers) while striving to beat your previous session. Consistency and effort beat novelty.
principles: Harder than last time: always beat the previous session in reps, weight, tempo, or reduced rest.
Train every body part at least twice per week (full-body, upper/lower, or a 3-day split by experience).
Control the tempo - about 2s down, 1s up, 1s pause - to maximize time under tension.
Target a rep range, not an exact number; stop warm-ups with 3-5 reps in reserve.
Sustainability and consistency beat short bursts of 'killing it' followed by burnout/injury.
Advanced 'all-out' sets and techniques (drop sets, partials, forced reps) are earned, not for beginners.
Do your cardio: about 150 minutes per week, also progressed 'harder than last time'.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 5-7 for the Hardcore split (Casual 2-3 full-body, Moderate 4 upper/lower)
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Light warm-up sets (12-20 reps) stopping 3-5 reps shy of failure; 10-30 min cardio + mobility first.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Straight sets to a target rep range with controlled tempo; only the final set of a movement goes 'all out' (advanced), optionally beyond failure with partials or a drop set.

splitOverview.focus: Run the 3-day split across 5-7 days so each muscle is hit 2x

sources.title: Harder Than Last Time Training Programs — Coach Greg Inc. (official)
Coach Greg YouTube (HTLT principles)
Greg Doucette Profile - Generation Iron

name: FST-7
creator: Phil Heath / Hany Rambod
summary: Fascial Stretch Training-7 (FST-7), created by coach Hany Rambod and made famous with Phil Heath: a body-part split with 3-4 straight working sets on most lifts, then a seven-set isolation finisher (8-12 reps, 30-45 seconds rest) to flood the muscle and stretch the fascia. Abs often first when trained. Heath typically trained five to six days per week with one major group (or pairing) per day.
principles: Finish each body part with FST-7 on an isolation move: 7 sets × 8-12, 30-45s rest.
Flex and stretch the target muscle between the sevens — the pump is the point.
Most other exercises: 3-4 working sets in the 8-12 hypertrophy zone.
Train five to six days on a traditional body-part split; recover a full week per large muscle.
Control every rep; use weight as a tool for the target muscle.
Abs first on days they are trained (per Rambod).

guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once per week for large muscles with FST-7.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 1-2 progressive warm-ups per exercise; abs first when included.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3-5 exercises with 3-4 working sets, then one FST-7 isolation finisher (7×8-12, 30-45s). Sessions ~70-90 minutes.

sources.title: FST-7 — Official (Hany Rambod)
8 Tips to Train Like Phil Heath - Muscle & Fitness (interview with Hany Rambod)
Training Style: FST-7 - Muscle & Fitness / Flex
Training with THE GIFT - Mr Olympia Phil Heath - Muscle & Fitness

name: Golden Era Volume
creator: Arnold Schwarzenegger
summary: Arnold Schwarzenegger's Golden Era approach from The New Encyclopedia of Modern Bodybuilding: enormous volume, agonist-antagonist pairing, and each major group twice per week on a six-day split — Chest & Back, Shoulders & Arms, Legs, then repeat. Early career used the Golden Six full-body template three non-consecutive days per week. Supersets, giant sets, and 8-12 (often higher) reps define the pump-first aesthetic of the era.
principles: Train each major body part twice per week on the competitive six-day split.
Pair antagonists (chest with back) so you can superset and keep density high.
Give shoulders and arms their own day so arms are fresh — not pre-fatigued from chest/back.
Use high volume: often 15-20+ sets for large parts across several angles.
Progressive overload with controlled form; chase the pump without ego form breakdown.
Golden Six is the beginner/early-career full-body alternative (3×/week).

guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Twice per week on the competitive split; 3×/week on Golden Six.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Progressive warm-ups on the first compound of each session.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Competitive split: typically 4-5 working sets per exercise, 10-20+ sets per large body part; supersets on chest/back and arms. Golden Six: 3-4 × 10 (AMRAP chin-ups/sit-ups).

sources.title: The New Encyclopedia of Modern Bodybuilding — Arnold Schwarzenegger & Bill Dobbins
Arnold's Golden Six Routine - Fitness Volt
Arnold Split overview - StrengthLog (Encyclopedia-based)
Tip: Train With Arnold's Golden Six - T Nation

name: Stimulate, Don't Annihilate
creator: Lee Haney
summary: Lee Haney's eight-Olympia philosophy — stimulate, don't annihilate. Favorite structure: three days on, one off (chest & arms; legs; back & shoulders; rest), with calves and abs every training day. Pyramid into working sets at ~75-85% max; pair push with pull when possible for joint longevity. Do not chase failure every session.
principles: Stimulate the muscle — do not annihilate it every workout.
Three on, one off gives each body part ~three recovery days.
Train calves and abs every training day.
Pyramid warm-ups into heavy work; top sets often 6-10 at 75-85%.
Protect joints — prefer push/pull pairings over stacking two press days.
Consistency and longevity beat one reckless all-out week.

guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once every four days in the cycle.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Pyramid warm-ups — light 15, then 10, then 8 before top work.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3-4 exercises per body part, often 4 working sets on compounds (6-10). Calves & abs every session. Avoid failure every set.

sources.title: Lee Haney's Training Split Suggestions - Muscle & Fitness / Flex
Workout Systems: Lee Haney's 4-Day Training Split - Poliquin Group
Lee Haney Workout: Top Training Tips - The Barbell

name: Growth Program
creator: Frank Zane
summary: Frank Zane's Growth Program (frankzane.com): a three-way split — Pull (back, biceps, forearms, abs), Legs (thighs, calves, abs), Push (chest, shoulders, triceps, abs). Used for the 1979 Olympia on a 3-on/1-off cycle; Zane now prefers a 5-5-5-6 recovery cycle (train 3 days out of 5 three times, then 3 out of 6) so you grow between sessions.
principles: Three-way split: pull, legs, push — symmetry over sheer mass.
Train abs every workout; include forearms on pull day.
Prefer more rest than a rigid 3-on/1-off forever — Zane's modern 5-5-5-6 cycle.
Moderate loads, strict form, inter-set stretching; vary order slightly each session.
Quality and proportion beat chasing the heaviest possible weight.
Log sessions; progressive overload with control.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Historically 3 on / 1 off; recommended now: 5-5-5-6 recovery cycle
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once per pull/legs/push rotation (extra rest on 5-5-5-6).
guidelines.warmupProtocol: One light warm-up set per exercise; never rush into heavy weight.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3-4 sets × 8-12 with moderate loads and strict form; stretch between sets on key moves.

sources.title: The Growth Program, Then and Now — Frank Zane (official)
Frank Zane Three-Way Training Split - Fitness Volt
The Legend of Frank Zane - SimplyShredded

name: One Step Closer Volume
creator: Jay Cutler
summary: Jay Cutler's One Step Closer / Olympia-era high-volume training: about 20 working sets per body part (typically 4–5 exercises × 3–4 working sets — warm-ups do not count), short 30–60s rests, and bodybuilding rep ranges that live in the 10–15 zone more than pure strength work. Filmed at Gold's through the 2005 Olympia and the weeks after — stacks of angles, controlled mind-muscle reps, and dense pumps. Scale down if you cannot recover from pro-level volume.
principles: Aim for ~20 working sets per body part — Jay's career standard (warm-ups and feel sets do not count).
Use 4–5 exercises and 3–4 working sets each; vary angles, not just load.
Rest ~30–60 seconds — speed training for metabolic stress and the pump.
Reps stay mostly in the 10–15 bodybuilding range; compounds can sit 8–12 when heavy.
Control every rep — contraction and stretch beat ego weight.
Rest after brutal back and leg days in the 3-on / 1-off / 2-on / 1-off rotation.

guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once per week; calves twice; abs often with shoulders.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 1–2 progressive warm-ups / feel sets on the first compound of the day (and when load jumps). Warm-ups are not part of the ~20 working-set target.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: ~20 working sets per body part: usually 5 movements × 4 working sets (or 4 × 3–4). Arms day hits both biceps and triceps near that volume each. Short rests; sessions run long.

sources.title: Jay Cutler — One Step Closer (Full DVD)
Jay Cutler — One Step Closer 1/5
Jay Cutler — One Step Closer 2/5
Jay Cutler — One Step Closer Part 4/5
Jay Cutler — Heir to the Olympia Throne (MESO-Rx / Ron Harris)
Jay Cutler on Sets Per Body Part — Fitness Volt

name: Lion of Lebanon
creator: Samir Bannout
summary: Samir Bannout — Lion of Lebanon, 1983 Mr. Olympia — trained on a hard 3-on/1-off cycle with old-school volume, short rests (~35s in prep), and heavy compounds. Public workout reconstructions vary; the sessions here follow commonly cited 1983-prep templates (shoulders & arms; legs & abs; chest & back) rather than a single surviving official program PDF. Treat set counts as high-volume era references and scale to recovery.
principles: Train hard when you train — short rests (~35 seconds) and heavy compounds.
Three days on, one off; fully recovered before repeating the cycle.
Listen to the body — adjust exercises and reps by feel, not only by numbers.
Build the back with rows, pulldowns, and Olympic-lifting roots for density.
Morning and afternoon sessions in peak prep to hit weak points twice.
Art and aesthetics matter as much as the weight on the bar.

guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once every four days in the cycle.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: One high-rep warm-up on the first compound (20-25 reps), then pyramid up.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 4-6 sets per exercise, 6-15 reps by feel; 35-second rest between sets in competition prep.

sources.title: Samir Bannout - The Lion of Lebanon - Fitness Volt
Samir Bannout's Workout and Diet - Iron and Grit Fitness
Old School Bodybuilder: Samir Bannout Workout - Nutribody

name: The Blade
creator: Dexter Jackson
summary: Dexter Jackson — The Blade — from his Iron Man Magazine interview: a five-day split after moving up from three days a week. Monday chest & abs, Tuesday back & calves, Wednesday/Thursday off, Friday quads, Saturday shoulders & arms, Sunday hamstrings. About 10 working sets per body part across 2-3 exercises historically; 3-4 sets on most lifts (5 on squats), mostly 6-10 reps with high-rep (up to 30) extensions/curls for detail, supersets often, forced reps sparingly.
principles: Train smart for longevity — heal injuries fully; ego does not own the session.
Compounds first (squats, bench, rows, chins) — the bread and butter.
Moderate volume: ~3-4 working sets per exercise; ~10 working sets per body part as a baseline.
Supersets often; forced reps only occasionally.
Separate quads and hamstrings when legs take too much out of one session.
Conditioning is identity — The Blade never missed peak sharpness.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 5 (Wed & Thu off in the Iron Man interview split)
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 2-3 warm-up sets on the first compound of each session.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3-4 working sets × 6-10 on most lifts (5 on squats). Leg extensions/curls can go very high-rep (up to ~30) for detail. Supersets common.

sources.title: The Blade — Iron Man Magazine (Dexter Jackson interview)
Dexter Jackson chest training - Fitness Volt / YouTube
Dexter Jackson on machines & longevity - Generation Iron

name: Annihilation Training
creator: Rich Gaspari
summary: Rich Gaspari's old-school intensity: pre-exhaust supersets, training to complete failure with drop sets and forced reps, then moderating the following week so muscles recover and grow. Four to five training days per week, each muscle once weekly, warm-up and cool-down rituals mandatory, and total focus in the gym — no phone, no distractions, war mindset on every set.
principles: Pre-exhaust isolation before compounds so the target muscle fails first.
Train to complete failure on intense weeks; follow with a moderate pump week.
Four training days minimum for recovery — six-day grinding stalls growth.
Warm up thoroughly and stretch after; longevity depends on joint care.
No distractions — total focus and intensity every working set.
Alternate annihilation weeks with moderate pump sessions for the same muscle.

guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once per week; intense week then moderate week.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Extended warm-ups and mobility before heavy work; cool-down stretches after.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Pre-exhaust: isolation to failure, then compound. Intense weeks use drop sets, forced reps, and supersets; next week moderate sets for blood flow and recovery.

sources.title: Pre-Exhaustion Shoulder Training — Gaspari Nutrition (official)
Rich Gaspari's Old School Chest and Back Superset — Gaspari Nutrition
Rich Gaspari on Training to Complete Failure - Fitness Volt
